coredata.dc:title: Thermochemistry of binary liquid Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Ivanov, M.I.
Bulanova, M.V.
Berezutski, V.V.
coredata.dc:description:
The eutectic type of phase diagram of the Tb–Ti system is confirmed, the eutectic temperature being 1215°C. The enthalpies of mixing of the liquid Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys were measured by isoperibolic calorimetry within a composition range up to 30at.% Ti (Gd–Ti system) and up to 50at.% Ti (Tb–Ti system). The limiting partial enthalpies of mixing of titanium are

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phase diagrams of Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti systems [4]: (▵) present study DTA at heating.
Mixing enthalpies (kJmol−1) in liquid Gd–Ti alloys at 1780K (standard state: liquid Gd and supercooled liquid Ti)
Mixing enthalpies (kJmol−1) in liquid Tb–Ti alloys at 1800K (standard state: liquid Tb and supercooled liquid Ti)

article.ce:title: Thermochemistry of binary liquid Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratoire de Physico-chimie de la Matiere Condensee, UMR-CNRS 5617, University of Montpellier II, Montpellier, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: I.N. Frantsevich Institute for Problems of Materials Science, Krzizanovski str. 3, Kyiv 03142, Ukraine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The eutectic type of phase diagram of the Tb–Ti system is confirmed, the eutectic temperature being 1215°C. The enthalpies of mixing of the liquid Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys were measured by isoperibolic calorimetry within a composition range up to 30at.% Ti (Gd–Ti system) and up to 50at.% Ti (Tb–Ti system). The limiting partial enthalpies of mixing of titanium are
Δ
H ¯
Ti
0
( kJ
mo
l
− 1
) = − 22.2 ± 2.0
at
1780
K
( Gd – Ti ) ,
Δ
H ¯
Ti
0
( kJ
mo
l
− 1
) = − 26.7 ± 3.4
at
1800
K
( Tb – Ti )

ce:section.ce:para: Binary alloys of titanium with lanthanides (Ln) are the boundary systems for multicomponent alloy materials for different technological applications: as high temperature in situ composite structural materials, as magnetic materials [1–3] etc. The row of Ln–Ti phase diagrams may be divided into two groups: the first one, with a miscibility gap in the liquid state (from La to Gd), and the second one, of eutectic type (from Tb to Lu), as has been recently shown in [1]. So, the Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti systems are the border systems of each group according to this classification. In this connection the investigation of these systems including thermochemical measurements and phase equilibria data is of significant theoretical and practical interest.

The Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti phase diagrams are shown in Fig. 1 . The Gd–Ti system is given according to Ref. [4]; the Tb–Ti phase diagrams is based on the prognosis reported in Ref. [4] and also includes the prognosis and the results of the present research.

Thermochemical data for the binary alloys of the lanthanides with titanium are scarce, the Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti systems remain unexplored in the liquid state. In this paper the experimental verification of the eutectic temperature in the Tb–Ti system and the results of a calorimetric study of the binary liquid systems Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti are presented.

The purity of the metals used in the experiments was 99.98% for Ti, 99.84% for Gd and Tb. The 50Tb–50Ti alloy was prepared by arc-melting and studied in as-cast state by metallography and differential thermal analysis (DTA). The experimental details have been described elsewhere [5].

The thermochemical experiments were carried out in the high temperature calorimeter described earlier [6] in purified helium medium under a pressure of 105 Pa at 1780–1800K. The experimental data of the heat of mixing (ΔH) were obtained for homogeneous liquid alloys within the concentration range up to 30at.% Ti at 1780K in the Gd–Ti systems and up to 50at.% Ti at 1800K in the Tb–Ti systems. Hafnium dioxide crucibles were used as melt containers. Molybdenum crucibles (purity 99.98%) were also used when dealing with lanthanide rich alloys.

During each experimental series, small samples of metals (masses within 0.01–0.03g) were dropped into the calorimetric bath, containing the pure metal (Gd or Tb) or the alloy melt (initial mass of the metal about 2.3g). For calibration we used pure metals (Gd, Tb) and Mo, as has been described earlier [6]. Molybdenum was used only at the end of the experimental series, because it might react with titanium in the melts. However, all the calibrations showed consistent data for the molar thermal equivalent (k) of the calorimeter. The thermal effect due to dissolution of a metal dropped into the calorimetric bath was calculated from the ΔT versus time curve by integration. The heat balance equation when dropping titanium is
k ∫
Δ
T ( t )
d t
=
Δ
H
Ti , 298
T
+
Δ
H
Ti
m
+
Δ
H ¯
Ti
where
Δ
H
Ti , 298
T
is the enthalpy for heating 1mol of Ti dropped into the bath from 298K to the bath temperature, taken from Ref. [7],
Δ
H
Ti
m
= 15.06
kJmol−1 is the melting enthalpy of Ti [8], and
Δ
H ¯
Ti
is unknown partial molar heat of mixing of Ti, referred to a supercooled liquid state, and t is the temperature relaxation time.

The sum of the experimental
Δ
H ¯
Ti
data points in the form of α-function (
α
Ti
=
Δ
H ¯
Ti
( 1 −
x
Ti
)
− 2
) was treated statistically by least-squares analysis using Forsythe orthogonal polynomials and is presented as a polynomial series.

The calculation procedure, based on the Gibbs–Duhem equation, also gives the smoothed values of both the partial enthalpies of the components and the integral enthalpy of mixing. Details of the calculation procedure were described previously [9].

The resulting equations for partial α-functions (kJmol−1) are
α
Ti
= − 22.21 − 36.72 x
( Gd – Ti , 1780
K )
and
α
Ti
= − 26.74 − 19.63 x − 138.88
x 2
( Tb – Ti , 1800
K )
where x = x Ti.

Smoothed calculated enthalpy data for Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti systems are listed in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. The reliability limit is equal to twice the standard deviation of the corresponding α-function.

The enthalpies of mixing in liquid Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys demonstrate small exothermic values, reflecting the weak chemical interaction between the components. These results are in agreement with the phase equilibria data for both the systems, including the present study, which confirmed the absence of the miscibility gap in the Tb–Ti systems. A microstructure of as-cast alloy 50Tb–50Ti (Fig. 2 ) shows the primary crystallization of 〈β-Ti〉 and eutectic 〈β-Ti〉+〈α-Tb〉. The eutectic temperature measured by us (1215°C) is somewhat lower than that (1270°C) reported in [4]. This is in better agreement with our estimation (1190±40°C), based on the linear correlation of the eutectic temperatures with the lanthanides melting points, as has been recently reported [1].

The enthalpies of mixing in the liquid alloys of lanthanides with 3d transition metals are known to be related to the electronic characteristics of the elements [10]. Traditionally, one suggests that the 3d band of a transition metal overlaps with the 5d band of a lanthanide by alloying to form a hydridized d band, which depends on the Fermi energy, the density of states and the bandwidth of the components [8,10]. For the lanthanides the density of states is much like that of transition metals, the bandwidths of d shell for titanium and the lanthanides are also very similar [8,10]. However, in the particular case of Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti systems, there is a marked difference between the Fermi energies of the components: about −3.15eV for Gd or Tb, and −3.95eV for Ti (estimates based on electron emission data [11]). Therefore, the Gd–Ti and Tb–Ti alloys are expected to be formed with an exothermic effect. The ΔH in the Tb–Ti system was found to be more exothermic than in Gd–Ti system: this is in agreement with a tendency of increasing of the absolute values of the mixing enthalpy through the lanthanides row, previously demonstrated for other series of binary liquid alloys of the lanthanides with 3d metals [6,9,12].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was performed within the bilateral Franco-Ukrainian program Dnipro and supported by Ukrainian Ministry of Science and Education and the Foreign Ministry of France, Project M/123-2003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selected Values of the Thermodynamic Properties of the Elements
